_flags: the "34 to 39" figure for 4A-C connecting rod cap nuts is listed under "1984" with a separate unlabeled "29" figure on the next line before "1986 and 1987...36" — the raw OCR draft doesn't clearly attach a year label to the "29" row. Reconstructed above as "1985" based on the visual table position (between the labeled 1984 and 1986-1987 rows), but this year assignment is inferred, not printed. Checked the p.87-p.90 torque-spec page images during review and could not locate this specific row to confirm — the year label may be on a page outside that range, or the OCR draft's table ordering may not match the source layout 1:1. diagnosis: This appears when none of the other codes are identified

_label: Code 2 — 2 Flashes, Pause, 2 Flashes — Air flow meter signal
diagnosis: Open circuit in Vc, Vs, Vb or E2
trouble-area: Air flow meter circuit; Air flow meter; ECU

_label: Code 3 — 3 Flashes, Pause, 3 Flashes — Ignition signal
diagnosis: No signal from igniter four times in a row
trouble-area: Ignition circuit (+B, IGf); Igniter; ECU

_label: Code 4 — 4 Flashes, Pause, 4 Flashes — Coolant temperature sensor circuit
diagnosis: Open or short circuit in the coolant temperature sensor or circuit
trouble-area: Coolant temperature sensor circuit; Coolant temperature sensor; ECU

_label: Code 5 — 5 Flashes, Pause, 5 Flashes — Oxygen sensor signal
diagnosis: Open circuit in oxygen sensor signal (lean indication only)
trouble-area: Oxygen sensor circuit; Oxygen sensor; ECU

_label: Code 6 — 6 Flashes, Pause, 6 Flashes — RPM signal
diagnosis: No Ne or G signal to ECU within several seconds after engine is cranked
trouble-area: Distributor circuit; Distributor–Igniter; Starter signal circuit; ECU

_label: Code 7 — 7 Flashes, Pause, 7 Flashes — Throttle position sensor signal
diagnosis: Open or short circuit in throttle position sensor signal
trouble-area: Throttle position sensor circuit; Throttle position sensor; ECU

_label: Code 8 — 8 Flashes, Pause, 8 Flashes — Intake air temperature sensor signal
diagnosis: Open or short circuit in intake air temperature sensor
trouble-area: Air temperature sensor circuit; ECU

_label: Code 10 — 10 Flashes, Pause, 10 Flashes — Starter signal
diagnosis: No STA signal to ECU when vehicle is stopped and engine is running at more than 800 rpm
trouble-area: Starter relay circuit; IG switch circuit (starter); IG switch–ECU

_label: Code 11 — 11 Flashes, Pause, 11 Flashes — Switch signal
diagnosis: Air conditioner switch ON, idle switch OFF, or shift position D range (automatic only) during diagnostic check
trouble-area: Air conditioner switch; ECU; Idle switch; Neutral switch (automatic only)

diagnosis: This appears when none of the other codes are identified

_label: Code 12 — 1 flash, Pause, 2 flashes — RPM signal
diagnosis: No "Ne" signal to the ECU within 2 seconds after the engine is cranked; no "G" signal to the ECU two times in succession when engine speed is between 500 rpm and 4000 rpm
trouble-area: Distributor circuit; Distributor; Starter signal circuit; Igniter circuit; Igniter; ECU

_label: Code 13 (EFI only) — 1 flash, Pause, 3 flashes — RPM signal
diagnosis: No "Ne" signal to the ECU when the engine speed is above 1500 rpm
trouble-area: Distributor circuit; Distributor; ECU

_label: Code 14 (EFI only) — 1 flash, Pause, 4 flashes — Ignition signal
diagnosis: No "IGF" signal to the ECU 4 times in succession
trouble-area: Igniter circuit; Igniter; ECU

_label: Code 21* — 2 Flashes, Pause, 1 Flash — Oxygen sensor
diagnosis: Problem in the oxygen sensor circuit
trouble-area: Oxygen sensor circuit; ECU

_label: Code 22 — 2 Flashes, Pause, 2 Flashes — Coolant temperature
diagnosis: Open or short in the temperature sensor circuit
trouble-area: Coolant temperature sensor circuit; Coolant temperature sensor; ECU

_label: Code 24 (EFI only) — 2 Flashes, Pause, 4 Flashes — Intake air temperature sensor
diagnosis: Open or short in the intake air temperature (THA) circuit
trouble-area: Intake air temperature sensor circuit; Intake air temperature sensor; ECU

_label: Code 25* — 2 Flashes, Pause, 5 Flashes — Air/fuel ratio lean malfunction
diagnosis: The air/fuel ratio feedback compensation valve or adaptive control valve remains at the upper (lean) or lower (rich) limit for a certain period of time
trouble-area: Injector circuit; Injector; Oxygen sensor circuit; Oxygen sensor; ECU; Fuel line pressure; Air leak; Air-flow meter; Air intake system; Ignition system; EBCV circuit (carburetor only); EBCV (carburetor only)

_label: Code 26* — 2 Flashes, Pause, 6 Flashes — Air/fuel ratio rich malfunction
diagnosis: The air/fuel ratio is overly rich
trouble-area: Injector; Injector circuit; Oxygen sensor circuit; Oxygen sensor; Fuel line pressure; Mass air-flow meter; Cold start injector; EBCV circuit (carburetor only); EBCV hose (carburetor only); EBCV (carburetor only); ECU

_label: Code 27 — 2 Flashes, Pause, 7 Flashes — Sub-oxygen sensor (California 4A-GE engine models only)
diagnosis: Open or shorted circuit
trouble-area: Sub-oxygen sensor circuit; Sub oxygen sensor; ECU

_label: Code 31 — 3 Flashes, Pause, 1 Flash — Vacuum sensor (4A-F, 4A-FE engine only)
diagnosis: Open or short circuit
trouble-area: Vacuum sensor circuit; Vacuum sensor; No vacuum to sensor

_label: Code 31 — 3 Flashes, Pause, 1 Flash — Air flow meter (4A-GE engine only)
diagnosis: Open or short circuit
trouble-area: Mass air flow circuit; Mass air flow meter; ECU

_label: Code 41 — 4 Flashes, Pause, 1 Flash — Throttle position sensor (EFI) or throttle switch
circuit-or-system: Throttle position sensor (EFI) or throttle switch
diagnosis: Open or short in the throttle position sensor circuit
trouble-area: Throttle position sensor or switch; Throttle position sensor or switch circuit; ECU

_label: Code 42 (EFI only) — 4 Flashes, Pause, 2 Flashes — Vehicle speed sensor
diagnosis: No "SPD" signal for 5 seconds when the engine speed is above 2800 rpm
trouble-area: Vehicle speed sensor circuit; Vehicle speed sensor; ECU

_label: Code 43 (EFI only) — 4 Flashes, Pause, 3 Flashes — Starter signal
diagnosis: No "STA" signal to the ECU until engine speed reaches 800 rpm with the vehicle not moving
trouble-area: Starter signal circuit; Ignition switch; Main relay–ECU

_label: Code 51 (EFI only) — 5 Flashes, Pause, 1 Flash — A/C switch signal
diagnosis: Air conditioner switch on, idle switch off during diagnosis check
trouble-area: A/C switch circuit; A/C switch; A/C amplifier; Throttle position sensor circuit; Throttle position sensor; ECU

_label: Code 52 — 5 Flashes, Pause, 2 Flashes — Knock sensor (1990 and later 4A-GE engines only)
circuit-or-system: Knock sensor (1990 and later 4A-GE engines only)
diagnosis: Open or short circuit
trouble-area: Knock sensor circuit; Knock sensor; ECU

_label: Code 71* — 7 Flashes, Pause, 1 Flash — EGR (California 4A-GE engine only) is too low <!-- NOTE: source misprint, not OCR — confirmed against the p.162 page image, printed exactly as "EGR (California 4A-GE is too low engine only)" in the "Circuit or system" column, with "is too low" oddly nested inside the parenthetical. The "Diagnosis" column ("EGR gas temperature signal") suggests the intended meaning is "EGR gas temperature is too low (California 4A-GE engine only)" — but transcribed here exactly as printed, not corrected. -->

The "Diagnosis" column ("EGR gas temperature signal") suggests the intended meaning is "EGR gas temperature is too low (California 4A-GE engine only)" — but transcribed here exactly as printed, not corrected. -->
diagnosis: EGR gas temperature signal

_label: Code 72 (carb only) — — — Fuel cut solenoid signal
diagnosis: Open circuit in fuel cut solenoid signal (FCS)
trouble-area: Fuel cut solenoid circuit; Fuel cut solenoid; ECU

text: Resistance: 0.5 – 2.0 Ω

With the temperature above 72°F, check operation of the valve by placing a finger over the atmospheric port and blowing into the port for the HIC diaphragm. Air should flow from the port normally connected to the carburetor.

At temperatures below 72°F, carry out a similar air flow test: place a finger over the port normally connected to the carburetor, blow through the port for the HIC diaphragm, and make sure air does not pass out of the atmospheric port.

_section: Common spark plug conditions (PDF p.290)
_label: Normal — Brown to grayish-tan color and slight electrode wear. Correct heat range for engine and operating conditions. — When new spark plugs are installed, replace with plugs of the same heat range.
symptoms: Brown to grayish-tan color and slight electrode wear. Correct heat range for engine and operating conditions.
recommendation: When new spark plugs are installed, replace with plugs of the same heat range.

_section: Common spark plug conditions (PDF p.290)
_label: Worn — Rounded electrodes with a small amount of deposits on the firing end. Normal color. Causes hard starting in damp or cold weather and poor fuel economy. — Plugs have been left in the engine too long. Replace with new plugs of the same heat range. Follow the recommended maintenance schedule.
symptoms: Rounded electrodes with a small amount of deposits on the firing end. Normal color. Causes hard starting in damp or cold weather and poor fuel economy.
recommendation: Plugs have been left in the engine too long. Replace with new plugs of the same heat range. Follow the recommended maintenance schedule.

_section: Common spark plug conditions (PDF p.290)
_label: Too hot — Blistered, white insulator, eroded electrode and absence of deposits. Results in shortened plug life. — Check for the correct plug heat range, over-advanced ignition timing, lean fuel mixture, intake manifold vacuum leaks, sticking valves and insufficient engine cooling.
symptoms: Blistered, white insulator, eroded electrode and absence of deposits. Results in shortened plug life.
recommendation: Check for the correct plug heat range, over-advanced ignition timing, lean fuel mixture, intake manifold vacuum leaks, sticking valves and insufficient engine cooling.

_section: Common spark plug conditions (PDF p.290)
_label: Carbon deposits — Dry sooty deposits indicate a rich mixture or weak ignition. Causes misfiring, hard starting and hesitation. — Make sure the plug has the correct heat range. Check for a clogged air filter or problem in the fuel system or engine management system. Also check for ignition system problems.
symptoms: Dry sooty deposits indicate a rich mixture or weak ignition. Causes misfiring, hard starting and hesitation.
recommendation: Make sure the plug has the correct heat range. Check for a clogged air filter or problem in the fuel system or engine management system. Also check for ignition system problems.

_section: Common spark plug conditions (PDF p.290)
_label: Preignition — Melted electrodes. Insulators are white, but may be dirty due to misfiring or flying debris in the combustion chamber. Can lead to engine damage. — Check for the correct plug heat range, over-advanced ignition timing, lean fuel mixture, insufficient engine cooling and lack of lubrication.
symptoms: Melted electrodes. Insulators are white, but may be dirty due to misfiring or flying debris in the combustion chamber. Can lead to engine damage.
recommendation: Check for the correct plug heat range, over-advanced ignition timing, lean fuel mixture, insufficient engine cooling and lack of lubrication.

_section: Common spark plug conditions (PDF p.290)
_label: Ash deposits — Light brown deposits encrusted on the side or center electrodes or both. Derived from oil and/or fuel additives. Excessive amounts may mask the spark, causing misfiring and hesitation during acceleration. — If excessive deposits accumulate over a short time or low mileage, install new valve guide seals to prevent seepage of oil into the combustion chambers. Also try changing gasoline brands.
symptoms: Light brown deposits encrusted on the side or center electrodes or both. Derived from oil and/or fuel additives. Excessive amounts may mask the spark, causing misfiring and hesitation during acceleration.
recommendation: If excessive deposits accumulate over a short time or low mileage, install new valve guide seals to prevent seepage of oil into the combustion chambers. Also try changing gasoline brands.

_section: Common spark plug conditions (PDF p.290)
_label: High speed glazing — Insulator has yellowish, glazed appearance. Indicates that combustion chamber temperatures have risen suddenly during hard acceleration. Normal deposits melt to form a conductive coating. Causes misfiring at high speeds. — Install new plugs. Consider using a colder plug if driving habits warrant.
symptoms: Insulator has yellowish, glazed appearance. Indicates that combustion chamber temperatures have risen suddenly during hard acceleration. Normal deposits melt to form a conductive coating. Causes misfiring at high speeds.
recommendation: Install new plugs. Consider using a colder plug if driving habits warrant.

_section: Common spark plug conditions (PDF p.290)
_label: Oil deposits — Oily coating caused by poor oil control. Oil is leaking past worn valve guides or piston rings into the combustion chamber. Causes hard starting, misfiring and hesitation. — Correct the mechanical condition with necessary repairs and install new plugs.
symptoms: Oily coating caused by poor oil control. Oil is leaking past worn valve guides or piston rings into the combustion chamber. Causes hard starting, misfiring and hesitation.
recommendation: Correct the mechanical condition with necessary repairs and install new plugs.

_section: Common spark plug conditions (PDF p.290)
_label: Detonation — Insulators may be cracked or chipped. Improper gap setting techniques can also result in a fractured insulator tip. Can lead to piston damage. — Make sure the fuel anti-knock values meet engine requirements. Use care when setting the gaps on new plugs. Avoid lugging the engine.
symptoms: Insulators may be cracked or chipped. Improper gap setting techniques can also result in a fractured insulator tip. Can lead to piston damage.
recommendation: Make sure the fuel anti-knock values meet engine requirements. Use care when setting the gaps on new plugs. Avoid lugging the engine.

_section: Common spark plug conditions (PDF p.290)
_label: Gap bridging — Combustion deposits lodge between the electrodes. Heavy deposits accumulate and bridge the electrode gap. The plug ceases to fire, resulting in a dead cylinder. — Locate the faulty plug and remove the deposits from between the electrodes.
symptoms: Combustion deposits lodge between the electrodes. Heavy deposits accumulate and bridge the electrode gap. The plug ceases to fire, resulting in a dead cylinder.
recommendation: Locate the faulty plug and remove the deposits from between the electrodes.

_section: Common spark plug conditions (PDF p.290)
_label: Mechanical damage — May be caused by a foreign object in the combustion chamber or the piston striking an incorrect-reach (too long) plug. Causes a dead cylinder and could result in piston damage. — Repair the mechanical damage. Remove the foreign object from the engine and/or install the correct-reach plug.
symptoms: May be caused by a foreign object in the combustion chamber or the piston striking an incorrect-reach (too long) plug. Causes a dead cylinder and could result in piston damage.
recommendation: Repair the mechanical damage. Remove the foreign object from the engine and/or install the correct-reach plug.
